item.title: Re: Staff Post - Wikiwalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
Sounds great! There are definitely some similarities there - and I love the idea of more crosslinks happening.

I say go for it, this sounds like a great change.

item.title: Staff Post - Wikiwalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

Damn this thing is a fun article. Anyway, there are noticeable similarities between this object and SCP-669. Both are objects that mess with gravity and physics and were constructed by artists. It's possible that one artist may have helped with the construction of the other, or vice versa. As such, I am recommending the following edits:

Changing this:

SCP-2597 was found in an apartment belonging to known anartist ███ ██████, during investigation of his involvement in the creation of SCP-███. The object was contained at time of recovery in a large glass jar, filled with liquid mercury. Once its anomalous properties were identified, the object was given SCP classification and a member of nearby Site 55's recovery division was dispatched to recover SCP-2597.

To this:

SCP-2597 was found in an apartment belonging to known anartist ███ ██████, during investigation of his involvement in the creation of SCP-669. The object was contained at time of recovery in a large glass jar, filled with liquid mercury. Once its anomalous properties were identified, the object was given SCP classification and a member of nearby Site 55's recovery division was dispatched to recover SCP-2597.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

item.title: Re: Falling upwards
item.content:encoded: …by your explanation, Person1871 is entirely right and I'm in the wrong to think that acceleration sign doesn't denote vector. I need to read more about it, I guess.

item.title: Re: Falling upwards
item.content:encoded:
+ You're both correct actually.
- SCIENCE!!!


I actually helped out with the initial math on this one, and one of the headaches involved was wrapping my head around the fact that there's actually two different types of mass: inertial mass1 and gravitational mass, which itself splits into active gravitational mass2 and passive gravitational mass3. In the real world these should always be the same, at least for the sort of matter that isn't named using greek letters and subscripts.4

If this were a true negative mass, Person1871 would be correct: the double-negative would cause negative mass to gravitate toward positive mass. This is fun in its own right because if you nail this sort of negative mass to a positive mass, you get something that looks like SCP-2052, constantly accelerating in the direction of the positive mass with zero net change in inertia.

A negative gravitational mass, meanwhile, would simply fly off into space without any of the really interesting behavior you get from negative inertial mass. Fun fact: Hydrogen and helium were thought to have negative gravitational mass for a few years after they were discovered, until it clicked for some people that two gasses could have the same pressure but different densities, and what they were seeing was buoyancy.

As the description points out, however, this SCP is an object with negative inertial mass- for the purposes of $F=G \frac{m_1m_2}{r^2}$, m is positive (it "weighs" 2 kg), but for the purposes of $a=\frac{F}{m}$, m is negative. The acceleration it experiences in gravity is normal, but the negative inertial mass effectively flips the vector to the opposite direction.

Footnotes
1. "body in motion"-type mass
2. "pulling on other stuff" mass
3. "getting pulled by other stuff" mass
4. People are really interested in said hypothetical matter because it'd let you make awesome stuff like Alcubierre Drives a whole lot more easily, and because it's a good way to liven up a physics conference with a fistfight or two.

item.title: Re: Falling upwards
item.content:encoded: Where is positive acceleration heading tho, I thought the sign of acceleration only indicates if the object is speeding up or slowing down? Or am I just forgetting how it works?

item.title: Falling upwards
item.content:encoded:
Won't the negative mass create negative force which cancels itself out leading to it still falling downwards?

i.e.

(1)
\begin{equation} F = Gm_1m_2 /r^2 \end{equation}

but since m1 is negative and m2 is the earth which has positive mass, F must also be negative, however

(2)
\begin{equation} a = F/m_1 \end{equation}

means the negative signs cancel therefore acceleration is positive meaning it still falls downwards.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah I knew I butchered some scientific terms. But yes, I get the point you are making about how the party closer to the black hole would suddenly spring backwards while the farthest point may not immediately, effectively smooshing it to what I would believe to be the infinitely thin pancake (instead of the thinnest noodle ever).

But even so, I realize that it is an impossibility for the skip to even possibly reach that level of gravitational pull (or push in this instance) as the it would most likely just deflect away due to how the skip functions in general.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A black hole actually has a theoretical infinite density, not an infinite mass. Acceleration of the object would be away from the black hole, but it would not achieve infinite velocity because it never experiences infinite gravity.

Admittedly, you'd see interesting effects from spaghettification. For normal matter near a black hole, the idea is that the part of an object closer in would feel a greater gravitational pull and accelerate faster than the part of the object further from the black hole.

In the case of the SCP, the region closer in would be accelerated outward faster than the region further away. You'd see a negative spaghettification, which I'll call a pancakeification. However, since by pancakeification, the object becomes denser and the distance from all parts of the object to the black hole are normalized, there would be a stabilization of the gravitational field across the object, with excess energy from the gravitational force most likely manifesting as heat (that depends on the chemistry of the negative mass, which has not been established).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
How would this SCP behave with black holes? Theoretically, a black hole has infinite mass, and therefore essentially an infinite gravitation pull. If we are to assume that this skip is to behave as the stronger the gravitation force, the higher acceleration it would go in the exact opposite direction, then theoretically we would have created a thing with infinite speed up to a point right?

Would it even be able to escape a black hole? If it doesn't, would it subtract mass from the singularity?

I'm sure I butchered the science with my statement, but I'm still curious as how it would behave in such situation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Whattaya mean? It…technically follows his laws of motion!

They just do weird things when you put minus signs where they aren't supposed to go.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This thing is pretty much the middle finger to Newton.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well it isn't clear if the Foundation coined it, but they use it. It is a contraction of "anomalous art".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I assume anartist is a piece of Foundation terminology?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nope, not at all. That got published about two days after I put this up, and I'm kinda irritated that it's made people assume there's a connection.

To be clear, this was not inspired by any recent event. This was not my attempting to shoehorn some new science into an SCP.

This is more like how Doctor Who used Weeping Angels shortly after 173 was written.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Because that's still putting an unbalanced force on the object, just through the mercury. It's not magic, it's just neutrally buoyant. Try to move the mercury jar and the skip comes flying out the back.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wait so why couldn't the agent carry the mercury jar instead of "carrying" the object itself? Wouldn't that be way easier to manage?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I guess this is related to this piece of news?

https://www.theguardian.com/science/2017/apr/19/scientists-have-created-a-fluid-with-negative-mass-but-what-does-it-tell-us

Anyway, very good precisely for its simplicity. +1 for sure.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I would say that depends on whether or not you applied equal and opposite forces, if you did it in your hand, it would certainly begin to accelerate in the direction with the highest force concentrated on it.

However, if it were done with equal and opposite forces, things get weirder. Normally when an object is subjected to a compressive load, elements within it push on each other and it deforms, becoming shorter in the loaded direction while it expands in the others(becoming shorter and fatter). An object subjected to a tensile load behaves in the opposite manner, becoming longer and thinner, a phenomenon which I trust you've seen once or twice.

I suspect that 2597 would behave as though it were in tension when it was compressed and vice versa.

item.title: HAHAHA, TIME FOR PHYSICS!
item.content:encoded:
Okay, I have to say that this is one of my favorite kinds of SCP, the ones that just break physics in some interesting way. I immediately thought of another one of my favorites, SCP-289 (though the math in its containment procedures has been shown to be off). On the one hand, I'd love to see cross-testing between them, but on the other, the results would be rather predictable.

+ Brief predictions of those reactions
- hide

SCP-2597, upon introduction to SCP-289's field of effect would have its inertial mass multiplied according to the following equation:

$m_t(t) = (-2)* 1.17^{t/160}$

As its inertial mass is negative this would make it increasingly reactive to outside forces. While it would be difficult to expose it for long enough, with the aid of other SCPs to contain and direct it, 2597 could theoretically be accelerated to an appreciable fraction of the speed of light depending on the course it took. It's also possible that it could exceed the speed of light depending on how the negative mass interacts with special relativity. (It could do this without 289's help, but I think it would make the process faster.)

Just saying, I would be lobbying for the immediate reproduction of this thing and the weaponization of such copies, relativistic weaponry is the stuff of nightmares.

I absolutely love it when an SCP gives me an excuse to work out its physics a little, this was awesome, keep up the good work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So…what happens if you squeeze it?

item.title: Re: My Second SCP
item.content:encoded: I might be cutting in here, but I followed your link to SCP-2917, and it's pretty good. I gave it a +1. I found the concept of what is essentially a two-item two-way portal to some other world to be very interesting, and I think you explored it well with the entry. Moreover, I like 2597 as well, though it does seem superficially similar to SCP-018: a red rubber ball that gains velocity with every bounce.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My only gripe is with the "nuclear blast" part. I'm not a nuclear physicist (yet), but "uncontrolled breakeven fusion of oxygen and nitrogen nuclei" seems like a big jump with this thing. To me, it feels like a sort of abrupt way to raise the stakes with something that's already awesome without the stakes being raised.

Then again, said reclassification does get denied, so maybe that was the point…? To show that an Agent's intuition isn't always correct?

Sorta. I'll admit that I did kinda want to raise the stakes a tad bit, but the main point of that exchange was exactly what you said - The guy was clever enough to bring it in and do his job, but he should leave the researching to the researchers.

On the other hand... it's definitely plausible for an object like this to do that. And given enough time to accelerate, it eventually would. The question is really more about whether it'd fall out of the atmosphere before it got to that point, and I didn't feel like doing some differential equations for a one-off bit in a skip.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As others have said, this is a fantastic article that takes a super simple concept and really goes into detail with how you'd actually have to work around said concept. Upvoted with enthusiasm for that, and I've always enjoyed seeing Foundation staff not being in mortal peril when trying to contain a skip, but just being plain frustrated.

My only gripe is with the "nuclear blast" part. I'm not a nuclear physicist (yet), but "uncontrolled breakeven fusion of oxygen and nitrogen nuclei" seems like a big jump with this thing. To me, it feels like a sort of abrupt way to raise the stakes with something that's already awesome without the stakes being raised.

Then again, said reclassification does get denied, so maybe that was the point…? To show that an Agent's intuition isn't always correct?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Looks like this SCP is featured on the front page. Good job!
Hey, congrats on the +100 yo! Great work!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: better without the addendum

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you! It's a tremendous honor - my second skip just broke 100!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You're welcome!

I'm the staff member who handles the front page features, yes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wait, are you the person who featured me? Thank you so much! That's awesome!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I can assure you that I didn't plan this in the slightest. Crazy!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yup. Way too much. The researchers plagiarized me, and I'm suing them./s

No, in all seriousness it's just weird as all hell. And it's made me kinda paranoid as well - how many of my upvotes here happened because of my skip, and how many happened because of the whole "release skip=>skip becomes real" story?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is it too much of a coincidence that this gets the featured page only a few days after this negative mass is actually discovered?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's a bit eerie if you ask me.

Random physicists: No! Some bloke on some fiction writing site has figured out my Nobel Prize-worthy achievement! Time to make my work public to claim the credit first!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh the bright side this weight exists without being at near absolute zero and lacking lasers.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah. I saw that.

On the one hand I'm excited about the new science, but on the other hand I'm kinda cheesed off that this happened literally two days after I put this skip up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
http://www.bbc.co.uk/news/science-environment-39642992

Just read this and thought of this scip.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Aha… Someone's been reading XKCD's 'What If?' articles.

I like finding references I get ;D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sorry I didn't do the math to work out what that sweet spot would be - I figured I'd spend lots of time working it out and that it wouldn't add all that much to the quality of the skip overall. Maybe sometime later.

And I'm not the one who came up with the contaiment - one of my brilliant friends suggested it, and some thinking on it suggested it could work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sirpudding! You gave me some awesome feedback on 2917, so I'm curious. what do you think of this one? An improvement?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not shoving it on the EX list - partly because I'm not a big fan of the concept of "Explained" skips and partly because this thing is still anomalous in that it exists as a solid object at room temperature.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, can't use it for wormholes or warpdrives, unfortunately.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yep, works perfectly now, thanks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Important caveat there: negative effective mass in highly controlled conditions. It's negative mass in the same way that a sonic black hole is a singularity. Still cool though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah- one thing I realized when I was working on the math for your buoyancy question on this is that it might be the only way to keep the thing in one place, long term.

It's free to drift around a little inside the mercury so you don't get feedback loops from constant accelerations like gravity and pressure, and so long as it stays close to that sweet spot and you don't bump it too much, it experiences a net normal force of zero. It goes deeper, it wants to sink back upward. It goes up, it wants to float back down.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Should this be updated to SCP-2597-EX now? :p

http://www.upi.com/Science_News/2017/04/17/Physicists-create-fluid-with-negative-mass/9381492444496/

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yup. Which, in the jar lid's case, would be downward. This would create an upward acceleration, pressing the object more strongly against the lid and increasing the normal force.

At some point the jar's gonna break. This is the reason why the containment procedures stipulate that the object must not contact the walls, ceiling, or floor - because any uncontrolled normal force could be deadly and result in loss of the object as well as several personnel.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh shit. All I know about the normal force is I had a really hard time with forces in college physics. D:

Is it because the normal force would be pushing upward, against the surface this thing is resting on? (c.f.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Changed the link to my original source instead of an imgur mirror. Better?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The science behind this makes my brain hurt. This was really good, and the interview was amazing. For some reason the image didn't load for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Cool. Instead of anti-matter, or anti-gravity, it's anti-force. We should do that with a couple other properties of physics. Negative heat? Anti-light? Negative length? Anti-time? Negative electricity? Anti-sentience?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a really smart way to turn a few old cliches into something quite satisfying. A nice example of something larger than its parts. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you! I'm frankly rather surprised by how this is doing myself - I thought it was a thoroughly mediocre idea when I started writing it.

The interview log even started out life as a cheap, tacked-on, "I need something else on this skip" sort of thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Probably not, but that's an entertaining image at least!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Maybe I'm not picturing this right, but I have this weird mental image of this agent doing a moonwalk with this thing in his hands.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Because the normal force would make the thing accelerate towards the lid of the jar, which would increase the normal force, which would break through the jar, the cieling, and anything else in its way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm dumb. Please explain why you can't just let this sit against the ceiling of a jar and be done with it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I enjoyed the description of how much of a pain in the ass this was to transport. It's an interesting concept that explores the implications of its effect, and the Agent's log was funny. The part I found the most amusing was that to the agent, this nature-bending impossible object was nothing more than the most annoying day of their career with the Foundation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah, that explains it. I think I was looking at it as having reversed gravity, not negative inertial mass. Thanks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If ever an SCP deserved to be featured, this is the one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well thought out but ultimately not at all interesting for me beyond the superficial. The anomaly reminds me a little of the Super Ball, and it's… okay, I guess, but certainly not enough to sustain an entire article on its own. But what else is there for me to really invest any interest in?

The agent's story of ingenuity in containing this thing? No, not really, because that's glossed over in lieu of "I'm going to explain in excruciating detail why this object is dangerous despite the reader being able to deduce what this property would mean for themselves," and if it was, I feel a tale format might have served it better, in that tales allow you to develop character and other things most mainlist articles miss out on.

"This thing could be really dangerous if we fucked up"? See above: when you spoil your main conceit in the sixth paragraph of your article ("It has been theorized that collisions with air particles at high speeds will cause SCP-2597 to accelerate uncontrollably.") it loses any of the intrigue we wouldn't have spoiled ourselves from just thinking about the effects a little bit.

It's an amusing concept in theory, but I'm going to downvote it for the same reason I'd downvote someone mainlisting a fanwiki article on how their magic system of choice works: it's an okay concept paired with pretty much no actual development beyond the initial conceit.

And before you ask, I'm entirely fine with Clef's Thaumatology tales, mainly because they're entertaining exposition backed up with a solid dose of atmosphere, and the interview's dry plotdumping failed to be either.
